steps.text: Now that you know where you are and what's around you, let's move between folders.

## cd — change directory

`cd` is a command that moves you into a different folder. Think of it like walking from one room into another.

Let's go to your Desktop folder.
Now check where you are with `pwd`:
See how the path changed? You're now inside Desktop. Let's see what's on your Desktop from the terminal:

## cd .. — go up one level

The two dots (..) mean "the folder above this one" — also called the "parent folder." If you're inside Desktop, the parent is your home folder.

`cd ..` moves you up one level.
Confirm you're back where you started:

## cd ~ — go home

No matter where you are, `cd ~` takes you back to your home folder. The ~ character (called "tilde") is shorthand for your home directory. You'll see it used everywhere.

## Absolute vs relative paths

When you typed `cd Desktop`, that was a "relative path" — relative to wherever you happened to be standing.

You can also use an "absolute path," which spells out the full address starting from the very top:

    cd /Users/yourname/Desktop

(Replace "yourname" with your actual username.)

The / at the very beginning is called the "root." Root is the top-level folder that contains everything on your computer. Every absolute path starts from root.

    /                        ← root (the top)
    └── Users/
        └── yourname/        ← your home folder (~)
            └── Desktop/     ← where we just were

Absolute paths start with /. Relative paths don't.

## mkdir — make a new folder

`mkdir` is a command that creates a new folder. The name stands for "make directory" (directory = folder).

First, make sure you're home:

You just created a folder using the terminal!

Try this: open Finder (on Mac) or your file manager and navigate to your home folder. You should see a folder called "practice" right there. You made that!

Now let's go into it:
Go back up to your home folder:

## rmdir — remove an empty folder

`rmdir` is a command that deletes an empty folder. It only works if there's nothing inside the folder.

Let's clean up the practice folder:

## What you learned
- `cd` moves you into a folder
- `cd ..` goes up one level to the parent folder
- `cd ~` goes to your home folder from anywhere
- Absolute paths start from root (/), relative paths don't
- Root (/) is the top-level folder on your computer
- `mkdir` creates a new folder
- `rmdir` removes an empty folder
